text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Multidisciplinary Tumor Board Review and Routine Oncology Visit

ATTENDING: Medical Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established patient follow-up / Post-Tumor Board Discussion

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC), now approximately 6 months into second-line cabozantinib therapy, presenting for routine follow-up and post-tumor board discussion. She reports increased afternoon fatigue but otherwise reasonable tolerance of her current regimen.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 64-year-old woman of West African descent, retired civil engineer, with a history of stage IV clear cell RCC (right kidney, pT3b pN2 M1) featuring 20% sarcomatoid differentiation and BAP1 loss. She was initially diagnosed approximately 18 months ago with a large right renal mass (11.4 cm) with extension into the right renal vein, retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy, and bilateral pulmonary metastases. She underwent open right radical nephrectomy with regional lymph node dissection (cytoreductive intent) followed by first-line combination ipilimumab plus nivolumab (Yervoy/Opdivo) per CHECKMATE-214. She achieved an initial partial response in her pulmonary disease but developed radiographic progression at approximately 9 months on therapy, with enlarging pulmonary nodules, an enlarging right pelvic sidewall lymph node, and new right external iliac adenopathy. Tumor NGS from the nephrectomy specimen had identified a MET amplification (copy number 7.2) along with VHL loss, BAP1 frameshift, PBRM1 splice site mutation, SETD2 mutation, and subclonal TP53 R248W. Based on this molecular landscape - and in particular the MET amplification - she was transitioned to second-line cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 60 mg daily.

Cabozantinib was initiated approximately 6 months ago. She was dose-reduced to 40 mg daily at week 4 due to grade 2 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome), with good response to dose reduction and urea-based cream. She also developed grade 2 diarrhea at week 6, managed with loperamide and dietary counseling, improving to grade 1. Grade 2 hypertension emerged at week 8; amlodipine was uptitrated from 5 mg to 10 mg daily with adequate blood pressure control thereafter. Restaging CT approximately 3 months into cabozantinib showed meaningful shrinkage of her pulmonary and pelvic nodal disease (roughly 27% reduction in total tumor burden from the pre-cabozantinib baseline). The most recent restaging CT at 6 months (reviewed today and presented at tumor board) shows stable disease - her pulmonary nodules are essentially unchanged (right lower lobe 1.8 cm, right upper lobe 0.9 cm, left lower lobe 1.1 cm), and her right pelvic sidewall and external iliac nodes are stable at 1.3 cm and 1.0 cm respectively. There are no new sites of disease.

She reports her energy has been somewhat lower than usual, particularly in the afternoons. She attributed this to her anemia, which was first noted at the 3-month visit. She is taking ferrous sulfate 325 mg on alternate days as initiated previously (alternate-day dosing selected given her CKD stage 3A). She denies worsening hand or foot pain. She reports 1-2 episodes per month of looser stools requiring loperamide, which she considers well within an acceptable quality-of-life range. She denies nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, mucositis, weight loss, or fevers. Blood pressure readings at home have been running 130-138/82-88 mmHg on amlodipine 10 mg. She has not noticed worsening shortness of breath and is comfortable walking 2-3 blocks. She denies hemoptysis, chest pain, pleuritic symptoms, or bone pain. No headaches, vision changes, or neurological symptoms.

Her hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily following ipilimumab/nivolumab-induced immune-related thyroid dysfunction, continues to be well controlled. Diabetes management has been stable on metformin.

From a psychosocial standpoint, she continues to see the oncology social worker on a monthly basis and describes strong family support. She remains engaged and motivated, expressing continued interest in any investigational therapies that may become available.

Case was reviewed today at our multidisciplinary genitourinary tumor board. Consensus recommendation was to continue cabozantinib 40 mg daily given ongoing disease control, acceptable toxicity profile, and the additional biologic rationale provided by MET amplification. The tumor board discussed that switching to a pure VEGFR-targeted agent (e.g., sunitinib or pazopanib) would be a step backward given the demonstrated activity of cabozantinib in this molecularly enriched context. No active clinical trial options are currently enrolling patients with this profile at our institution, though the team will continue to monitor for appropriate trial opportunities.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Fatigue grade 1-2, primarily in afternoons. No fevers, chills, or unintentional weight loss.
- HEENT: No headache, visual changes, hearing loss, or mucositis.
- Respiratory: No worsening dyspnea at rest. Mild exertional dyspnea with significant exertion (>3 blocks, stairs). No cough, no hemoptysis.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or peripheral edema. Blood pressure well controlled per home readings.
- Gastrointestinal: Occasional loose stools (1-2 days/month above baseline). No nausea, vomiting, abdominal cramps, rectal bleeding. No significant mucositis.
- Genitourinary: No gross hematuria, dysuria, or urinary obstruction symptoms. Baseline CKD well established.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild bilateral wrist and finger arthralgias, grade 1, persistent but not worsening (noted since IO therapy; likely residual immune-mediated arthralgia). No new joint swelling or reduced range of motion.
- Skin: Bilateral palmar and plantar erythema and mild desquamation, grade 1. Well managed. No new rash.
- Neurological: No focal deficits, headache, or cognitive changes.
- Endocrine: No symptoms of hypothyroidism or hyperthyroidism. Well controlled on levothyroxine.
- Psychiatric/Psychosocial: Mild situational anxiety related to disease course, supported by social work involvement. Mood generally stable.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 136/82 mmHg, heart rate 76 bpm, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 36.8°C, oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Weight 78.1 kg (BMI 28.2, stable from prior visit).

General: Well-appearing woman, alert and oriented x3, in no acute distress. Appears mildly fatigued but conversational and interactive.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis or thrush. No oral ulcers. Conjunctivae mildly pale, consistent with mild anemia. Sclerae anicteric.

Neck: No cervical, supraclavicular, or axillary lymphadenopathy. Thyroid not enlarged.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1, S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Respiratory: Mildly decreased breath sounds at the right base (chronic, consistent with prior surgery and residual metastatic disease). No wheezes, rhonchi, or egophony. Respiratory effort unlabored at rest.

Abdominal: Well-healed right flank surgical incision without dehiscence, hernia, or infection. Abdomen soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly palpable. No palpable masses.

Extremities: Bilateral palms and plantar surfaces with mild erythema, mild dryness, and fine desquamation of the palmar surface of both hands and heels bilaterally - grade 1 by CTCAE. No blistering or open wounds at this time. Capillary refill brisk. No lower extremity edema.

Neurological: Grossly intact. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits. Gait steady.

Skin: No new rash. Prior immune-related dermatitis fully resolved. Mild scattered xerosis.

Lymphadenopathy: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy in superficial nodal chains.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained at today's visit):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6.1 x10^9/L (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 10.8 g/dL (grade 1 anemia; baseline pre-cabozantinib was approximately 12.1 g/dL)
- MCV: 79 fL (microcytic, consistent with iron deficiency component)
- Platelets: 198 x10^9/L (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L
- Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.62 mg/dL (eGFR estimated ~44-45 mL/min/1.73m2, CKD stage 3A; stable from last visit)
- BUN: 26 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL
- AST: 29 U/L (normal)
- ALT: 31 U/L (normal; previously grade 1 elevation at 3-month visit, now normalized)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 72 U/L (normal)
- Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
- Glucose: 108 mg/dL (fasting)

Iron Studies:
- Serum iron: 44 mcg/dL (low)
- TIBC: 388 mcg/dL (elevated)
- Transferrin saturation: 11% (low)
- Ferritin: 18 ng/mL (low; confirms iron deficiency as a contributing etiology to anemia)

Thyroid Function:
- TSH: 2.4 mIU/L (normal, stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg)
- Free T4: 1.1 ng/dL (normal)

Hemoglobin A1c: 7.4% (well controlled; stable from prior)

Coagulation: Not obtained at this visit.

Urinalysis: Trace proteinuria (1+), consistent with CKD and TKI-associated proteinuria. No hematuria. Will recheck protein:creatinine ratio at next visit to ensure stability.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed today):

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast (most recent, obtained approximately 6 months after cabozantinib initiation, 15 months from initial diagnosis):
Pulmonary metastases remain stable. The dominant right lower lobe nodule measures 1.8 cm (slight fluctuation from 1.9 cm, within measurement variability), essentially unchanged. Right upper lobe nodule 0.9 cm, stable. Left lower lobe nodule 1.1 cm, stable. The bilateral smaller pulmonary nodules are stable or minimally smaller compared to the pre-cabozantinib baseline. The right pelvic sidewall lymph node is stable at 1.3 cm. Right external iliac adenopathy stable at 1.0 cm. No new pulmonary, hepatic, osseous, or adrenal lesions identified. Reviewed at tumor board today - overall impression is maintained disease control; the disease does not appear to be growing. The prior partial response to cabozantinib (first 3 months of therapy) has been consolidated into stable disease.

Prior MRI brain (obtained at time of progression on IO therapy): Negative for intracranial metastases. Per tumor board discussion, annual surveillance MRI brain is planned given the high-risk features of this tumor (sarcomatoid differentiation, BAP1 loss, MET amplification). Next brain MRI to be scheduled in approximately 6 months.

---

ASSESSMENT:

1. Stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma with sarcomatoid differentiation (20%), currently on second-line cabozantinib 40 mg daily - stable disease. This patient has derived meaningful and sustained benefit from cabozantinib after progressing through first-line IO-based combination therapy (ipi/nivo). The MET amplification identified on NGS provides a mechanistically coherent basis for cabozantinib's activity (VEGFR2/MET/AXL inhibition). Current disease burden: bilateral pulmonary metastases and right pelvic/external iliac nodal disease, all stable or slightly improved relative to the pre-cabozantinib baseline. She is tolerating the reduced dose of 40 mg daily well after initial grade 2 hand-foot syndrome and diarrhea necessitated dose reduction.

2. Grade 1 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (hand-foot syndrome), improved from prior grade 2. Well managed with urea-based topical therapy. No dose escalation or further reduction warranted at this time.

3. Grade 1 diarrhea, intermittent, well managed with loperamide on an as-needed basis. No dose modification required.

4. Hypertension, controlled on amlodipine 10 mg daily. Blood pressure at target range.

5. Grade 1 anemia, multifactorial - combination of TKI-related effect, CKD stage 3A (eGFR ~44-45), and iron deficiency (confirmed by low ferritin, low transferrin saturation, microcytic indices). Currently on alternate-day ferrous sulfate 325 mg. Hemoglobin stable at 10.8 g/dL but has not yet recovered. Increased fatigue is attributable in part to this. Will consider intravenous iron infusion if oral supplementation is insufficient over the next 6-8 weeks; patient's CKD and likely ongoing occult GI losses from TKI warrant this consideration.

6. Immune-related hypothyroidism, managed with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH stable at 2.4 mIU/L. Continue current dose.

7. Type 2 diabetes mellitus, well controlled. HbA1c 7.4% on metformin. Continue current management.

8. CKD stage 3A, stable. Creatinine 1.62 mg/dL at present, slightly elevated from pre-treatment baseline of approximately 1.45 mg/dL (in the context of having lost the right kidney). Trace proteinuria noted today - will formally quantify protein:creatinine ratio at next visit. Monitor closely; TKI-related proteinuria and nephrotoxicity are potential contributors.

9. Mild residual arthralgias (bilateral wrists, fingers), grade 1, likely residual immune-related arthritis from prior IO therapy. No active inflammatory synovitis on exam. Low-dose NSAIDs avoided given CKD. Continue monitoring; would consider rheumatology referral if symptoms worsen.

10. Psychosocial: Active engagement with oncology social work. Patient and family well informed and engaged in care. Goals of care: continued active treatment with priority on disease control and quality of life. Palliative care discussed as available and complementary to active oncologic treatment; patient acknowledges and has deferred formal referral at this time.

---

PLAN:

1. Continue cabozantinib (Cabometyx) 40 mg orally once daily. No dose change at this time given stable disease and acceptable tolerability.

2. Anemia management: Continue alternate-day ferrous sulfate 325 mg. Given slow trajectory of hemoglobin recovery and ongoing contributing factors (CKD, TKI), will schedule IV iron infusion (ferric carboxymaltose or low-molecular-weight iron dextran per pharmacy/infusion center availability) if hemoglobin does not improve to >11.5 g/dL at next visit in 4 weeks. Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents not appropriate at this time (active malignancy, no dialysis-level CKD). Will recheck CBC in 4 weeks.

3. Renal function monitoring: Obtain spot urine protein:creatinine ratio at next visit. If significant proteinuria (>1 g/g), consider nephrology referral and potential TKI dose hold/reduction discussion.

4. Blood pressure: Continue amlodipine 10 mg daily. Patient instructed to maintain home blood pressure log. If readings consistently exceed 150/95 mmHg, will add low-dose ACE inhibitor (acknowledging CKD and potential for creatinine fluctuation; will monitor closely).

5. Thyroid: Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH to be checked at next routine labs (4 weeks).

6. Diabetes: Continue metformin. HbA1c to be rechecked in 3 months. Note: metformin dose remains appropriate at current eGFR ~44-45; will hold if eGFR falls below 30.

7. Restaging imaging: Next CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 3 months.

8. Brain surveillance: Schedule MRI brain with and without gadolinium in approximately 6 months per tumor board recommendation given high-risk tumor biology.

9. Hand-foot syndrome: Continue urea 40% cream twice daily to bilateral palms and heels. Reinforce moisture-barrier sock use and avoidance of tight footwear. Maintain podiatry follow-up.

10. Diarrhea: Continue loperamide PRN. Reinforce dietary guidance (low-residue diet, adequate hydration). No dose change needed.

11. Clinical trials: Case to be re-reviewed for trial eligibility at the next tumor board. Patient will be notified of any new opening trials, including potential MET-directed or next-generation RCC combination studies.

12. Palliative care: Option discussed and remains open; patient declines at this time. Social work follow-up to continue monthly.

13. Next oncology office visit: 4 weeks for labs (CBC, CMP, TSH, urine protein:creatinine ratio) and clinical assessment. Full restaging visit in approximately 3 months.

All findings, the tumor board recommendations, and the plan were discussed with the patient and her adult daughter who accompanied her today. Questions were addressed. Patient verbalized understanding and agreement with the plan.

Time spent counseling and coordinating care: approximately 45 minutes.

Electronically signed,
Attending Physician, Medical Oncology
Genitourinary Oncology Program
